concept_name: Aircraft Ground Velocity — Adding Airspeed and Wind Vectors
source_book: DC Pandey 6.13 — aircraft-wind relative velocity; split from aircraft_wind_problems

real_world_anchor.primary: An Indigo A320 departing Delhi heading true north to Chandigarh at 250 km/h airspeed. A steady jetstream blows WEST at 50 km/h. The plane's nose points due north, but its ACTUAL ground track is north-northwest — it drifts west at 50 km/h while flying north at 250. Ground velocity is the vector sum: a resultant of magnitude sqrt(250^2 + 50^2) ≈ 255 km/h, angled arctan(50/250) ≈ 11.3 degrees west of true north. Every airline pilot in India plans for this on every departure.
real_world_anchor.secondary: An ISRO test drone flying east across a Bengaluru research campus at 10 m/s airspeed. A 4 m/s south wind blows across the field. Ground velocity = (10, -4), magnitude 10.8 m/s at arctan(4/10) = 21.8 degrees south of east. The drone's flight-controller constantly computes this vector.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A hot-air balloon rising over Pushkar at the sunrise festival. Balloon's vertical airspeed 2 m/s up, wind 5 m/s east. Ground velocity = (5, 2) — the balloon drifts east at 5 m/s while climbing at 2 m/s. Passengers watch the city slide east beneath them even though the flame points straight up.

formulas.core_formula: V_plane/ground = V_plane/air + V_air/ground    (airspeed vector + wind vector)
formulas.perpendicular_wind: If heading is perpendicular to wind: |V_ground| = sqrt(v_air^2 + v_wind^2)
formulas.headwind: Head-on wind (opposite to heading): |V_ground| = v_air - v_wind  (slower ground speed)
formulas.tailwind: Wind in same direction as heading: |V_ground| = v_air + v_wind  (faster ground speed)

constraints: Wind vector is always given in ground frame.
Airspeed vector is the pilot's SELECTED heading, not the ground track.
Ground velocity is what actually happens — what radar and GPS see.

STATE_1.title: Hook — Why Flight Path Differs from Heading

scene_composition.text:
Indigo flight Delhi -> Chandigarh, clear morning:

  Airspeed (pilot selects): 250 km/h  due NORTH
  Jetstream wind:            50 km/h  toward WEST

  What does RADAR on the ground see?

       N
       ^
       | 250                         V_ground (resultant)
       |                              /
       |                             /
       |                            /
       |__________ W 50           /
       |<---  wind

  Ground velocity = airspeed vector + wind vector

     V_ground  =  (0, +250)  +  (-50, 0)
                =  (-50, +250)

  That is a flight track 50 km/h to the WEST
  and 250 km/h to the NORTH simultaneously.

tts_sentences.text_en: An Indigo A320 takes off from Delhi, nose pointing true north at 250 kilometres per hour airspeed.
But there is a 50-kilometres-per-hour jetstream blowing west. So what does ground radar actually see?
Not pure-north motion. The plane drifts west while moving forward — the ground velocity is a vector sum.
Airspeed vector plus wind vector equals ground velocity. The plane ends up travelling north-north-west, a bit off the nose heading.

scene_composition.text:
Magnitude and direction of V_ground:

    V_ground  =  (-50, +250) km/h

    Magnitude  =  sqrt( 50^2 + 250^2 )
                =  sqrt( 2500 + 62500 )
                =  sqrt( 65000 )
                ~=  255 km/h  (slightly faster than airspeed because
                                wind is perpendicular, not head-on)

    Angle west of true north:
       tan(alpha)  =  50 / 250  =  0.2
       alpha       =  arctan(0.2)  ~=  11.3 deg

    Ground track: N 11.3 deg W, at 255 km/h.
    The ATC marks this as the 'over ground' speed.

tts_sentences.text_en: The resultant vector is 50 west and 250 north — minus 50 comma plus 250.
Its magnitude is the square root of 2500 plus 62500 — 255 kilometres per hour — slightly more than the pure airspeed because the wind is perpendicular.
Its direction is arctan of 50 over 250 — 11.3 degrees west of true north.
Ground track: N 11.3 degrees W at 255 kilometres per hour. That is the speed Air Traffic Control sees on radar.

STATE_3.title: Three Special Cases — Headwind, Tailwind, Crosswind

scene_composition.text:
Special cases worth memorising:

   HEADWIND (wind opposite to heading):
     V_ground =  v_air  -  v_wind    (minimum ground speed)

   TAILWIND (wind same direction as heading):
     V_ground =  v_air  +  v_wind    (maximum ground speed)

   CROSSWIND (wind perpendicular to heading):
     V_ground =  sqrt(v_air^2 + v_wind^2)
     plus drift at angle arctan(v_wind / v_air)

   GENERAL:
     V_ground =  sqrt(v_air^2 + v_wind^2 + 2*v_air*v_wind*cos(theta))
     where theta is the angle between the two vectors.

   Delhi example above is pure CROSSWIND case.

tts_sentences.text_en: Three special cases come up in every aircraft-wind question.
Headwind — wind against you — ground speed is airspeed minus wind speed. Minimum. Tailwind — wind helping you — ground speed is airspeed plus wind. Maximum.
Crosswind — wind perpendicular to heading — ground speed is the square root of v air squared plus v wind squared, with drift.
The general case uses the law-of-cosines formula with the angle between them. All three special cases are just particular angles.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student thinks the pilot-selected airspeed is the same as the ground speed — ignores wind altogether.
confusion_patterns: do I need wind for ground speed

STATE_1.title: Ignoring Wind is the Classic Mistake

scene_composition.text:
Student's shortcut:
  "The pilot selected 250 km/h airspeed.
   So the plane must be moving at 250 km/h over ground."

  This treats airspeed and ground speed as the same.

  They are NOT.

  Airspeed is the speed relative to the AIR.
  Ground speed is the speed relative to the GROUND.
  The difference is the WIND.

  Ignore wind -> predictions are wrong by as much as 20%
  on a high-altitude flight.

tts_sentences.text_en: A student sees airspeed 250 and immediately says ground speed is 250. Wind ignored.
Wrong. Airspeed is the plane's speed through the AIR; ground speed is its speed over the GROUND.
The difference is the wind's own motion. On a jetstream flight this difference can be 20 percent or more.

scene_composition.text:
The discipline that fixes this:

   Always write the chain with subscripts:

     V_plane/ground  =  V_plane/air  +  V_air/ground

   V_plane/air       -> pilot-selected airspeed vector
   V_air/ground      -> wind vector (reported by met office)
   V_plane/ground    -> what radar actually sees

   Add the VECTORS, not the magnitudes.
   Result uses the law of cosines for the magnitude
   and the arctan for the direction.

   This chain works for ALL frame problems,
   not just aircraft.

tts_sentences.text_en: The discipline that fixes this forever — write the chain rule with subscripts every time.
V plane slash ground equals V plane slash air plus V air slash ground.
V plane slash air is the pilot's selected airspeed. V air slash ground is the wind. V plane slash ground is what radar sees.
Always add the vectors, never the magnitudes. This chain rule works for every frame problem, not just aircraft.

scene_composition.text:
Tailwind example you can memorise:

   Mumbai -> Bengaluru, westerly jetstream (tailwind):
      v_air   = 200 km/h  east
      v_wind  =  40 km/h  east (same direction)

   V_ground = 200 + 40 = 240 km/h

   So the trip is 20% faster than if there were no wind.
   This is why east-bound long-haul flights in India
   get to destination EARLIER than timetable during
   the NE monsoon jetstream season.

   If you had ignored wind, you would predict
   trip time 20% longer than reality.

tts_sentences.text_en: One tailwind example to remember forever.
Mumbai to Bengaluru flight — airspeed 200, tailwind 40 in the same direction. Ground speed is 240.
Trip is 20 percent faster than the still-air case. This is exactly why east-bound long-haul flights across India arrive early in the northeast monsoon season.
